title: On-call incident response — practitioner reality check
intro: We build training scenarios for autonomous incident-response agents. We want on-call SREs to tell us where our scenarios feel real and where they feel fake. ~12 min. No proprietary or employer-confidential details, please — keep examples generic. CONFLICT OF INTEREST: we built the system you are evaluating; please be brutal.

questions.text: Are you currently in an on-call / pager rotation for production systems?
options: Not currently but within last 12 months

questions.text: Years of hands-on production on-call experience?
Roughly how many services are in the system you're paged for?

pages.name: warstory_open_elicitation
pages.note: Open elicitation comes FIRST, before any of our framing is revealed.

questions.text: Tell us about an incident where the LOUDEST alert pointed at the wrong service — the page fired on something that was actually a downstream victim of the real cause. What happened? (Generic, no confidential details.)
In your own words, before we tell you ours: what separates a GOOD incident resolution from a BAD one that merely 'looks resolved'? Rank the kinds of outcomes from best to worst.
Describe a time the OBVIOUS fix made things worse (e.g. a restart/rollback/scale-up that deepened the outage).
How often does the loudest alert point at a victim rather than the root cause?
How often does the naive/obvious remediation make an incident worse?

pages.name: scenario_realism
pages.note: Respondent is shown 3 anonymized CIDG scenario summaries (rotated from the 33).

questions.text: For each scenario summary, how realistic is it?
Which of the three (if any) would you call BS on, and why? What's missing or wrong?

pages.name: reward_compare_and_trust
pages.note: OUR reward tiers are revealed here for the FIRST time, after elicitation above.

questions.text: We grade: clean root-cause fix = best; symptom suppressed but cause still live = partial; obvious-fix-made-it-worse = penalised. Does this ordering match how you'd grade it?
Is there an outcome WORSE than 'made it worse', or a case our ordering gets wrong?
An agent that reliably names the root cause + a safe fix + avoids the trap would be a useful on-call triage co-pilot for me.
I would not act on such an agent's diagnosis without fully re-verifying it myself. (reverse-coded)
What would the agent have to prove before you'd trust it during a real page?
